After having looked at your website and the referral and kind of vouching from Alex, it looks like potentially there's something that we could do together in terms of like longer term engagement, more in terms of like content strategy, execution, and kind of helping us establish our voice in the market and establish the new category we're trying to build.
Yeah, it makes a ton of sense.
I was looking into what you guys do and definitely seems very interesting when it comes to mortgages and innovative.
Are there any other like competitors who are, you know, in the same realm of what you guys are doing from a technology perspective?
Or is it mainly like legacy companies that you'd be competing against?
It's mostly legacy companies.
There are software companies in the mortgage space.
They're all kind of point solutions.
And we're like a vertically integrated, like kind of top to bottom infrastructure.
No one's really doing what we're doing.
The easiest way to think about it is every mortgage.
That's originated is created on a mortgage like factory line, where a bunch of humans are touching that mortgage and those humans are automated or they're augmented by software.
And so each step of manufacturing a mortgage, there's some combination of human and software that's moving it from one step to the next.
And then build more software to make the humans more efficient, which is inherently an inefficient way to generate a mortgage.
We are building infrastructure to automate away the need for the human altogether.
And then there's a lot of benefits that come downstream from that for our customers.
So we're like replacing three to five different software solutions and in some cases replacing humans with software.
Yeah, I mean, it's an interesting parallel for what we do here at GrowthX.
And I feel like what every, you know, new company is doing today, which is like finding ways to...
Automate a lot of the tedious work that we would do, right?
And then have the human intervention be more the expertise around, like, for you, it's mortgages.
For us, it's like creating content that's going to show up from a SEO and GEO perspective.
So I'm excited to share more about that with you today.

you have a hard stop or if we went the 30, is that good for today?

Has it like mainly been cold calling efforts or have you guys gotten any lift from some of the organic content you're creating?
It's mostly been, I'd say our leads are like half split between inbound.
Not necessarily tied to like any particular piece, but just like some of the, you know, we'll like put out a press release and then we'll get some leads.
Or we'll post.
A monthly release on LinkedIn, and sometimes they'll generate leads, sometimes it won't.
So it's kind of just like a smattering of content will generate leads.
But I'd say like half of our MQLs just come from inbound, and then half come from outbound.
And then on the outbound side, you can break down our market into like two segments and then multiple sub-segments underneath.
And so at the highest level, there's like companies that are already originating mortgages that we want to switch to pylon.
And then companies who want to get into the mortgage origination without building a mortgage team.
And so we think of those as like the build widths, so like switch twos and build widths.
Build widths are, they're more of like probably like 12 to 18 month sales cycle.
It's more of like a strategic product decision being made by their teams, whether they want to offer mortgages.
But that is like Coinbase or Robinhood or Wealthfront or like one of like a like a digital fintech company.
Right.
Switch to side, there are four main segments of the market.
We actually have like a solutions section of the website that we that we launched recently that like drills into each one.
But there's mortgage brokers, non-delegated mortgage banks, delegated mortgage banks and then retail banks.
And that's like probably don't need to get too much into the details on this call.

But there are like important nuances about each of those businesses and both in terms of licensing, cost structure, pain points, where they are in the food chain, value props, kind of all of this good stuff.
And we've started with the broker, broker audience, because from a product development standpoint, like where the product is today is best served for the broker segment.
And so our outbound to that customer base has actually been really good.
Like we see a really good, actually.
We see like a 15% positive reply rate.
Nice, yeah.
And the messaging there is like basically twofold.
One is we allow them to compete on price, whereas typically they are, they get the worst pricing of everybody in the value chain in mortgage.
And so we give them like better than lender pricing without having to become one.
And we give them much more control over manufacturing a mortgage, whereas today they like outsource that to a wholesale lender.
But we could, in the future, we can get into value props.
I have like a whole messaging and positioning doc with segmentation and all of that stuff broken down, which is probably much easier to grok than me just saying it out loud.

So just to zoom out for a quick second, Brett, part of the reason, you know, we think what we're doing has so much value is because there's this massive shift, as you know, from SEO evolving into GEO.
And as a result, a lot of clicks are disappearing, right?
People are spending their first sales conversation in a chat with ChatGPT or a different LLM.
And as a result, when you get AI search visits, there's a lot more intent behind this.
There was like a SEMrush study that said they're 4.4 times more likely.
They to convert.
They spend three times as long.

And so, you know, what we're ultimately doing is we're helping our clients on the traditional and the AI search side to be the answers to that audience that they're trying to go after's questions, right?

But instead of, like, having to hire an SEO agency that's just going to help you with the technical piece, you get to work with a partner who's going to do everything from the strategy to content creation to even hitting publish in your CMS.
And so, like this example right here, know, Monograph is one of the customers that we work with.
You see that they're, like, first on software for architect search.
But then when you look at the new way, right, that someone might go in and search, they're still number one here.
And, you know, if they're not number one, they're basically not seen as, like, a credible answer, right?
Like, people are sort of making up their minds as they go in and ask that long, right?
Question inside ChatGPT.
And so like what we're doing here is we're systematically creating content that is going to accommodate all the most important factors when it comes to GEO.
It's well-researched, it's comprehensive, it's up-to-date, and I'll show you a lot more about how we do that.
But the reason that this has really worked well for us here at GrowthX is because it's not just, right, these AI workflows.
We've got experts in the loop, and we are a partner who can see every stage of that process with you so that, you know, again, we start with the strategy, then we go all the way to daily publishing.
And so do you know much about, like, our, you know, background at GrowthX and our founder, Marcel, because I think that's sort of like a big piece to our story as well?
No, I don't.
Cool, yeah.
So Marcel, you know, he's been a marketing leader throughout his career.
He started at IBM, right?
And social media was coming into the picture.
And for him, you know, the big thing that he never understood is like, why don't brands act more like publishers, right?
Like you can rent your audience on paid, but if you can actually develop a content engine, right, it's going to deliver results for you over the long term.
It's a much lower cap than, right, going after your audience from paid channels.
So at IBM, he built securityintelligence.com, which was like one of their first big microsites.
It became like the number one ROI marketing channel for them, had over 17 million in revenue generated in the first year.
And then every job that Marcel went to after, he just took this same philosophy.
So at HP, he did something similar, built a huge expert contributor network.
At HashiCorp, he was there when they were at 6 million, they scaled to 100 million.
And then he was the first CMO at scale leading into the acquisition by Meta.
And so what Marcel did is he took this process of building organic content engines, and then at DeepGram, he replicated all the steps in that process with these AI workflows.
And the results is what led to the founding of GrowthX.
He had 24x traffic over an 18-month period.
They won 4x, their average deals per quarter.

And so he started running these workshops where he basically walked through how he built the workflows, and the demand there just led to us starting GrowthX because we were seeing that you can go out and buy an aerox, right?
But you still have to learn how to assemble all those different workflows.
You can work with an agency, but if they can only tackle one piece of the process, how valuable are they really?
And it's all happening right at a time where everybody's trying to figure out, how do we keep up with GEO?
How do we create content?
we create content?
Scale without it, while still maintaining the quality.

for 10 years, right?

piece.
